1.synthesis: The peace offering may be from the herd, male or female, “without blemish” (v.1); the worshiper lays his hand on its head, it is slaughtered at the tent entrance, its blood thrown against the altar, and the fat portions and organs burned “as a food offering with a pleasing aroma to the LORD” (v.5). The integrative thread is fellowship and communion with God: unlike the wholly-burned ascension offering, the peace offering is largely eaten in a shared meal, celebrating reconciled relationship — the fat, the richest part, given to God, while worshiper and priest partake, picturing the joy of being at peace with the LORD.
1.historical_context: The peace offering (Hebrew shelamim, related to shalom) was the only sacrifice the worshiper shared in eating (Leviticus 7:15–16), making it a covenant fellowship meal. That a female animal was acceptable (v.1) widened the provision. The fat (Hebrew chelev), considered the choicest part, was reserved entirely for God (Leviticus 3:16), as was the blood — both belonging to the LORD alone. It was offered in thanksgiving, for vows, or as a freewill gift.
1.literary_structure: The peace offering follows burnt and grain offerings, completing the trio of “pleasing aroma” offerings before the chapter turns (in ch. 4) to the sin and guilt offerings that deal expressly with transgression. The shared structure — hand-laying, slaughter, blood thrown, fat burned (vv.2–5) — links it to the burnt offering, while its distinctive feature, the burning of the fat with the rest eaten, sets it apart as the offering of communion.
1.theological_application: The peace offering celebrates reconciled fellowship with God, the shalom that flows from atonement (Romans 5:1, “we have peace with God”). It teaches that the goal of sacrifice is not merely the removal of guilt but restored communion, expressed in shared joy at the table (Leviticus 7:15). That the choicest fat is given wholly to God reminds the worshiper to render him the best, even in the offering of thanksgiving, and to delight in fellowship with the God who has made peace. The peace offering crowns the sacrificial sequence by showing that the goal of atonement is restored communion, not merely cleared guilt (Romans 5:1). God invites the reconciled worshiper to a shared meal in his presence, a foretaste of the fellowship for which we were made. That the choicest fat is reserved for God within this joyful offering teaches that even our celebrations of communion with him are to render him the best and the first (Proverbs 3:9).
1.christology: Christ “is our peace” (Ephesians 2:14), who “made peace by the blood of his cross” (Colossians 1:20), fulfilling the peace offering by reconciling us to God. The shared meal of the shelamim foreshadows the communion believers enjoy at the Lord’s Table (1 Corinthians 10:16–18), feeding on Christ in fellowship and joy. Through him the atonement issues in peace and table fellowship with God, consummated at the marriage supper of the Lamb (Revelation 19:9).

6.synthesis: The peace offering may also come from the flock, a lamb or goat “without blemish,” offered with the same ritual of hand-laying, slaughter, and the burning of the fat and choice portions “as a food offering to the LORD” (vv.6–11). The integrative thread is the same communion with God made available through a humbler offering: the fellowship celebrated in the peace offering is open to those of more modest means, and the LORD’s claim on the fat — “all fat is the LORD’s” (Leviticus 3:16) — runs through every form, teaching that the best always belongs to God.
6.historical_context: As with the burnt offering, the flock provision (v.7) accommodated worshipers who could not bring cattle. The detailed specification of the fat, kidneys, and the long lobe of the liver to be burned (vv.9–10) reflects the careful priestly handling of the choicest internal portions, which were God’s alone. The lamb of the flock points back to the Passover and forward to the Lamb of God.
6.literary_structure: This section parallels the herd peace offering (vv.1–5) with the variation of animal, following the same descending-cost pattern seen in the burnt offering. The repeated emphasis on the fat as “a food offering with a pleasing aroma” (v.11) and the climactic statute “all fat is the LORD’s” (Leviticus 3:16) frame the chapter around God’s claim to the best.
6.theological_application: God’s reservation of the fat — the richest portion — for himself (Leviticus 3:16) teaches that he is worthy of the choicest of all we have, not the remnants (Malachi 1:8). The availability of fellowship through the flock offering again shows God’s gracious inclusion of all worshipers in the joy of communion. The believer learns that peace with God and the privilege of his table are open to all who come on his terms, and that he deserves our best in grateful devotion. The availability of fellowship through a humbler flock offering again declares God’s gracious inclusion of all worshipers in the joy of his table, regardless of means (Luke 14:21–23). And the recurring reservation of the fat for God (Leviticus 3:16) presses home that even in the offering of thanksgiving and communion, the Lord is worthy of the choicest portion, never the leftover or the second-best (Malachi 1:8).
6.christology: The lamb of the flock offered for peace foreshadows Christ, the Lamb led to the slaughter (Isaiah 53:7), whose blood makes peace and whose offering opens table fellowship with God to all, rich or poor. The choicest portions given wholly to God anticipate the Son’s offering of his very best — himself, without reserve — to the Father (Hebrews 9:14). In him the communion meal of peace is spread for all his people (Luke 22:19–20).

12.synthesis: The peace offering may be a goat, offered with the same procedure, the fat and choice portions burned “as a food offering with a pleasing aroma. All fat is the LORD’s” (v.16). The chapter closes with a perpetual statute: “you shall eat neither fat nor blood” (v.17). The integrative thread is the binding, lasting claim of God on the life (blood) and the best (fat) of every offering: across all animal types the same двух principles hold, and the closing prohibition makes them a permanent ordinance, teaching Israel that life and the choicest belong to God alone.
12.historical_context: The goat completes the range of acceptable peace offerings (v.12). The perpetual ban on eating fat and blood (v.17) had deep significance: blood represented life, which belongs to God and is the means of atonement (Leviticus 17:11), and the fat was the choicest portion reserved for him. This “statute forever throughout your generations” bound Israel’s diet to a daily acknowledgment of God’s claims.
12.literary_structure: The goat section rounds off the threefold provision (herd, flock, goats), and the chapter culminates in the summary statute (vv.16–17) that gives the theological rationale for all that preceded: “all fat is the LORD’s” and blood is forbidden. This closing ordinance ties the peace-offering laws to the broader sacrificial theology of blood and atonement unfolded in chapter 17.
12.theological_application: The enduring prohibition of blood and fat (v.17) trained Israel to reverence life as God’s and to render him the best, a discipline woven into ordinary eating. The reservation of blood for atonement (Leviticus 17:11) points to the seriousness of life and the costliness of forgiveness. Believers, no longer bound by the dietary statute (Acts 10:15; Mark 7:19), still honor its meaning: life belongs to God, and he is worthy of our choicest devotion. The perpetual statute against eating fat and blood (v.17) wove a daily reminder into Israel’s very meals: life belongs to God, and he claims the best. Though believers are freed from the dietary law (Mark 7:19; Acts 10:15), its abiding lesson stands — reverence for the sanctity of life and the giving of our choicest devotion to the Lord who gave his own life-blood for us (1 John 3:16).
12.christology: That blood belongs to God as the means of atonement (Leviticus 17:11) finds its fulfillment in the blood of Christ, “the life” poured out for the forgiveness of sins (Matthew 26:28; Hebrews 9:22). The fat wholly given to God prefigures the Son’s holding nothing back in his self-offering. The eternal statute pointed to an eternal redemption: Christ has secured by his own blood what the offerings could only foreshadow (Hebrews 9:12).
